title: Obviously Awesome vs Crossing the Chasm: Head to Head Comparison
metaDescription: Compare Obviously Awesome vs Crossing the Chasm. Detailed head-to-head comparison with strengths, weaknesses, and expert verdict. Which book is right for you?

book1.title: Obviously Awesome
book1.subtitle: How to Nail Product Positioning so Customers Get It, Buy It, Love It
book1.author: April Dunford
book1.description: You know your product is awesomeâ€”but does anybody else? Forget everything you thought you knew about positioning. Successfully connecting your product with consumers isn't a matter of following trends, comparing yourself to the competition or trying to attract the widest customer base. April Dunford's Obviously Awesome shows you how to use a simple, structured process to position your product so that prospects see it as truly awesomeâ€”and genuinely want to buy it.

book2.title: Crossing the Chasm
book2.subtitle: Marketing and Selling Disruptive Products to Mainstream Customers
book2.author: Geoffrey A. Moore
book2.description: Here is the bestselling guide that created a new game plan for marketing in high-tech industries. Crossing the Chasm has become the bible for bringing cutting-edge products to progressively larger markets. This edition provides new insights into the realities of high-tech marketing, with special emphasis on the Internet. It's essential reading for anyone with a stake in the world's most exciting marketplace.

table.book1: Product positioningâ€”how to articulate what makes your product different and valuable
table.book2: Market adoptionâ€”how to cross from early adopters to mainstream market

table.book1: Your positioning is weak and customers don't understand your value
table.book2: You have early adopters but can't break into the mainstream market

table.book1: Product marketers, founders, anyone defining product positioning
table.book2: Tech companies scaling from early adopters to mass market

table.book1: Clarifying positioning for any product at any stage
table.book2: Understanding why adoption stalls and how to cross into mainstream

book1Strengths: The 10-step positioning process is brilliantly practical and actionable
Modern (2019)â€”reflects current market realities and customer expectations
April Dunford's experience positioning 16+ companies shows throughout
Shorter at 224 pagesâ€”more focused and digestible
Works for any product, not just high-tech
The positioning canvas is a fantastic tool you can use immediately
Higher rating (4.6 vs 4.4) shows strong practical impact
Examples span B2B SaaS, consumer products, and services
book1Weaknesses: Doesn't address market adoption dynamics beyond positioning
Less focus on the scaling and growth challenges
Doesn't explain why products fail to cross into mainstream
More tactical than strategicâ€”doesn't cover market timing
Assumes you have product-market fit already
book2Strengths: The definitive book on technology adoption and market dynamics
The Chasm concept has become part of startup vocabulary
Larger audience (3,200 vs 1,800) shows broader influence
Explains why so many great products fail to scale
The bowling pin strategy for market entry is brilliant
Essential reading for understanding market dynamics
The whole product concept is invaluable for product strategy
Has stood the test of time for 30+ years
book2Weaknesses: Published 1991â€”many examples (workstations, PDAs) feel very dated
Longer at 320 pages with some repetitive sections
The technology focus may not translate to all industries
Lower rating (4.4 vs 4.6) suggests some readers find it less actionable
Doesn't provide a step-by-step positioning framework
Can feel theoretical rather than immediately practical
Some concepts need translation to modern markets

book1: Positioning is the act of deliberately defining how you are the best at something that a defined market cares a lot about.
Your best features are not your positioning.
Positioning isn't what you say about yourself; it's what the market understands about you.
If you don't deliberately position yourself, the market will do it for youâ€”and you won't like the result.
The goal of positioning is to control the story about your product in the market.
Good positioning makes it obvious why your product is different and worth paying for.
Position your product in a market where you can credibly claim to be the best.
book2: The chasm represents the gulf between two distinct marketplaces for technology productsâ€”the first being an early market dominated by early adopters, and the second a mainstream market dominated by the early majority.
Visionaries and pragmatists have very different expectations, and they need to be marketed to very differently.
To cross the chasm, you must focus exclusively on achieving a dominant position in one or two narrowly bounded market segments.
The number one corporate objective, when crossing the chasm, is to secure a distribution channel into the mainstream market.
If you try to cross the chasm without a target market focus, you will fall into it.
Whole product means the complete set of products and services needed to achieve the compelling reason to buy.
The bowling pin strategy: knock over one pin, then use that success to knock over the next.
Pragmatists buy from market leaders, not from technology leaders.

book1: Get the 10-step process for nailing product positioning
Learn why best features don't equal good positioning
Master the positioning canvas tool for immediate implementation
Understand how to articulate differentiated value clearly
Perfect for product marketers and founders positioning products
Modern framework that reflects current market dynamics
book2: Understand the Technology Adoption Lifecycle and the Chasm
Learn why products with early traction fail to scale
Master the bowling pin strategy for market entry
Discover the whole product concept for mainstream success
Essential for tech companies crossing into mainstream markets
The definitive book on market dynamics and adoption

verdict.summary: Obviously Awesome wins if your challenge is positioningâ€”customers don't understand your value or what makes you different. The 10-step process is more actionable and modern. Crossing the Chasm wins if you have early adopters but can't break into mainstreamâ€”it explains market dynamics and adoption. Dunford is about messaging; Moore is about market strategy. Different problems, different solutions.
verdict.recommendation: If you're struggling with positioning and customers don't 'get' your product, start with Obviously Awesome. April's framework is immediately actionable and will clarify your messaging. If you have early traction but sales have stalled at the chasm, read Crossing the Chasm to understand market dynamics and how to reach pragmatists. For tech founders, read both: Obviously Awesome for positioning, Crossing the Chasm for market strategy. If you only read one and positioning is your issue, choose Obviously Awesomeâ€”it's more tactical and modern. Both are essential, just at different stages.
